zh: 在食堂吃完饭，姜海慢悠悠的走在回家的路上。
他租的房子离厂区也不算太远，步行回去也花不了多少时间。
边走边回想着和李国强、陈小亮他们的闲谈。
姜海逐步清晰了一些事情。
"目前这个世界的时间线是 地球历2015年5月"
"这和我原先所处的时间线不对，我记得是 蓝星历2029年5月"
"而且，虽然是2015年了，但世界格局、国家大事、所在城市等等完完全全的不同"
"并不是穿越到过去，所以以往那些成功经验如果放在现在实施，多半是行不通的"
姜海回忆起一些穿越小说里主角掌握了一些关键信息从而一度崛起的桥段就感到好笑。
这世界哪有那么好改变？通过上一世的经验总结去影响到这一世，简直就是个笑话。
"首先，新闻方面就是平淡无奇"
姜海默默思索着，通过这段时间的回忆，姜海发现，这个世界好像有点不太对，怎么个不对法暂时说不上。
但他记忆里的各种事情倒是让他很奇怪，虽然也有各个国家，但近数百年貌似并没有什么大的冲突和战争。
记忆里历史课里，几百年前有一次世界格局的突变后，各个国家就相安无事了，从农耕经济、到工业经济顺利过渡。
姜海所在的龙国也没有什么百年屈辱史，没有什么外来入侵，反抗历史，统统没有。
新闻里每天播报的无非就是天气预报和全国气候情况。
离谱的是天气情况几乎占据着整个新闻播报，主持人讲天气细致到哪个城市那个地区会不会下雨。
这就有点过分了。
也没啥大事情，最严重的也就是哪个学校的小孩子数学考了100分之类的。
"其次就是手机问题"
作为上一世紧跟技术潮流的码农，姜海对于手机应用的开发还是比较上心的。
目前所处的地球的手机和他的蓝星完全不是一个级别，姜海记得蓝星2015年手机已经发展的很好了，大屏幕智能机已经很普遍。
但是，目前的地球2015年手机虽然也有，但不是必需品，很多人都不用手机，功能也非常单一，屏幕也小，黑白屏居多，居然连彩色屏的也是少之又少。
电脑也发展缓慢，大多数公司企业用的还是老式的286类型的电脑，开个机等半天，内存才可怜的4M，硬盘也就200M。
这让姜海一度以为这个地球其实是90年代初的水平。
手机、电脑等发展滞后，导致游戏也发展不起来，别说什么王者、吃鸡等玩不到，连俄罗斯方块也只能用小霸王学习机玩。
"我要是以后大力发展电脑、手机游戏，会不会发达的很？"
姜海有点开心，作为码农，写代码开发个游戏还是可以的，虽然他一时半会儿做不出什么3A大作，但做一个动作类的游戏起步，然后慢慢组建团队，招募人手，发展壮大，最后弄一个世界知名的游戏开发公司，他还是有点信心。
不过，很快，姜海打消了这个想法，因为……
没有群众基础。
貌似这个世界的人对游戏并不太感兴趣。
"再看看吧"
边想着边走
此时姜海路过一个十字路口，对面的红灯亮着，姜海习惯性的站着等。
周围行人车辆不多，稀稀拉拉的。
姜海从小就被教育不许闯红灯，哪怕周围没车辆，没警察，都不许。
何况，现在姜海的正前方不远处，正好有个警察。
这个警察远看就很壮实，头戴头盔，戴着墨镜，也不知道他在看什么。
"哒哒哒哒"
一阵急促的脚步声引起了姜海的注意，他回头看去，只见一个身着西装的男人疾步向他走来。
然后这个西装男也不管红灯，径直向前走去。
姜海像看傻瓜一样看着这个西装男，心里想："这货那么急？没看到前面有警察？"
目视着西装男穿过人行道横道线，立刻被警察拦下，姜海心里涌现了幸灾乐祸的心情。
只见戴墨镜的警察拦住了西装男的去路，伸手敬礼，语气生硬的说到：
"您好，你刚才闯红灯了！"
西装男站着不耐烦的说到："是的，我赶时间，麻烦让一下，我下次注意，我……"
一声枪响。
此时，姜海被这突如其来的枪响吓了一哆嗦。
只见西装男已经头部中弹，后仰倒在地上，
与此同时，
墨镜警察已经把手枪收了回去。
姜海的震惊下巴已经快要落到了地上。
什么情况？原主的记忆里并没有闯红灯要被击毙的情况啊。
这个警察那么勇的吗？
一言不合就开枪？
还有王法吗？还有法律吗？
震惊之余，姜海发现自己的系统视觉上弹出一些字幕。
【附近：警察***执行'制止闯红灯行为任务'，击杀了1名犯罪人员，任务进度1/5】
"看来这个世界的人也会接到任务"
"警察的名字看不见，可能是一种保护措施"
姜海不由感叹。
同时，他看着这个警察也发现个细节，他头上悬浮一行不易察觉的文字和条框，仔细看是1/5的字样。
这说明有任务的人头上有进度。
此时，戴墨镜的警察看了看倒在地上的西装男，觉得再守这个路口不一定有什么收获了，
他也不管周围聚拢过来的人，骑上巡逻摩托，一个油门轰鸣，扬长而去，估计去别的路口蹲守了。
"居然有如此离谱的任务，这以后我要是接到杀人任务咋办"
"不杀的话，应该会被消除，杀的话又违背自己的内心"
码农姜海是连鸡都没杀过的宅男。
【系统，查阅一下当前的交通法规，是否有闯红灯要被击毙的条款】
【宿主您好，目前所处城市交通法规，第8条明确规定，警察有权制止一切闯红灯行为，可以使用武术、枪击、狙杀等方式击毙犯罪人员】
"不想了，以后发生的事情以后再说"
姜海看了看已经绿灯了，匆匆过了马路，避开了西装男流了一大摊的血迹，快步的向家里走去。
因为他的脑海视觉提示，系统分给他的任务就快要开始了。
en: After finishing his meal at the cafeteria, Jiang Hai took his sweet time strolling home.
His rented place wasn't too far from the factory—walking back wouldn't take long at all.
As he walked, his mind replayed the lunchtime chatter with Li Guoqiang and Chen Xiaoliang.
Piece by piece, things were starting to crystallize in Jiang Hai's head.
*So the current timeline is May 2015, Earth Calendar.*
*That doesn't line up with my original world. I remember May 2029, Blue Star Calendar.*
*And even though it's 2015—the geopolitical landscape, major national events, even the city I'm in—everything's completely different.*
*This isn't time travel. None of my past-life hacks will work here.*
Jiang Hai caught himself smirking as he remembered those cliché transmigration novels—the protagonist remembering some key detail and using it to snowball into a meteoric rise. As if.
As if the world was that easy to bend. Trying to game a new world with old-world experience? What a joke.
*First thing that stands out: the news is painfully boring.*
Jiang Hai chewed on the thought. Over the past few days of tapping into his new memories, he'd sensed something was... off. He couldn't put his finger on it yet.
But the historical record was genuinely bizarre. Sure, there were countries. But for the past several hundred years? No major conflicts. No wars worth mentioning.

From what he remembered of history class, a world-changing event a few centuries back had somehow reset the global order. After that, every nation just... got along. Smooth transition from agrarian to industrial economy, no drama.
Long Country—his new homeland—had no Hundred Years of Humiliation. No foreign invasions. No resistance epics. Nada.
The daily news broadcasts? Weather forecasts and national climate reports. That's it. That's the whole show.

The truly insane part: weather took up nearly the entire broadcast. The anchors would break down precipitation patterns to a granular level—like, "Will it rain in this specific district of this specific city?"
Come on. That's just overkill.
And nothing else ever happened. The most earth-shattering headline of the week might be some kid in some school scoring a perfect 100 on a math test.
*Second thing: the phone situation.*

In his previous life, as a coder who lived and breathed tech, Jiang Hai had a sharp eye for mobile app development. Blue Star in 2015 had already seen the smartphone revolution in full swing—big screens, smart features, ubiquitous.

But this Earth's 2015? Phones existed, sure, but they were hardly essential. A huge chunk of the population didn't even use them. Features? Rudimentary. Screens? Tiny. And most of them were black-and-white. Color screens were a goddamn rarity.

Computers weren't much better. Most businesses were still running those ancient 286-style dinosaurs. You could make a cup of tea in the time it took to boot one up. A pathetic 4MB of RAM. A hard drive clocking in at 200MB.
It honestly felt like this Earth was stuck somewhere in the early 1990s.
With phones and computers this primitive, gaming was basically nonexistent. Forget about Honor of Kings or PUBG—you couldn't even play Tetris without a knockoff Famiclone console.
*If I pushed hard into computer and mobile gaming... would I hit it big?*

The thought lit Jiang Hai up. As a coder, he could absolutely build a game from scratch. Sure, he wasn't going to crank out a AAA blockbuster overnight, but he could start with an action game, then slowly recruit a team, expand the operation, and eventually build a world-famous game studio. He had the chops.
But almost as quickly as the thought arrived, he shot it down. Because...
There was no audience.
People in this world just... didn't seem to care about games. At all.
*Let's just see how things play out.*
He kept walking, lost in thought.
Jiang Hai reached a crosswalk just as the light turned red. Out of sheer habit, he stopped and waited.
The streets around him were sparse—only a few pedestrians and vehicles trickling by.
He'd been drilled since childhood: never run a red light. Didn't matter if there wasn't a car in sight or a cop within a mile. You just didn't do it.
And right now? There was definitely a cop. Standing maybe twenty meters ahead.
Even from this distance, the officer looked solid—broad-shouldered, helmet on, sunglasses hiding his eyes. No telling what he was staring at.
*Tap-tap-tap-tap.*
The rapid footsteps snapped Jiang Hai out of his thoughts. He turned to see a man in a suit striding toward him at a brisk clip.
The suit didn't even glance at the red light. He just kept walking—straight into the crosswalk.
Jiang Hai stared at the guy like he was an absolute idiot. *What's his rush? Can't he see the cop right there?*
He watched as the suit crossed the crosswalk—and was immediately intercepted by the officer. A delicious wave of schadenfreude washed over Jiang Hai.
The sunglasses-wearing cop blocked the suit's path, raised a hand in a crisp salute, and spoke in a flat, mechanical tone:
"Sir. You just ran a red light."
The suit shifted impatiently. "Yeah, I'm in a hurry. Just let me through. I'll be more careful next time. I—"
*BANG.*
A gunshot.
Jiang Hai physically flinched. The sound slammed into his chest like a thunderclap.
The suit was already on the ground—shot through the head, slumped backward, pooling red on the asphalt.
And in the same heartbeat,
the sunglasses cop had already reholstered his pistol.
Jiang Hai's jaw nearly hit the ground.
What the actual hell? His inherited memories sure as shit didn't mention getting executed for running a red light.
Was this cop just... built different?
Zero words, straight to gunfire?
What happened to due process? What happened to the law?
As the shock reverberated through his skull, Jiang Hai noticed his system interface flickering with new text in the corner of his vision.
[Nearby: Officer *** executing 'Red Light Interdiction Task'. One violator neutralized. Task progress: 1/5.]
*So people in this world get assigned tasks too, huh.*
*Can't see the cop's name. Probably some kind of privacy protection.*
Jiang Hai let out a low whistle under his breath.
Then he noticed another detail: floating above the cop's head was a barely perceptible string of text and a progress bar. On closer inspection, it read 1/5.
So people on active tasks had visible progress bars over their heads. Good to know.

The sunglasses cop glanced down at the crumpled suit, seemed to decide this intersection wasn't going to yield any more action, and ignored the growing crowd of onlookers. He swung a leg over his patrol motorcycle, gunned the engine with a throaty roar, and peeled off—probably to stake out another intersection.
*What kind of deranged mission system is this? What if I get assigned a kill order someday?*
*If I refuse, I'll probably get... erased. But going through with it? That's crossing a line I can't uncross.*
Let's be real: Coder Jiang Hai was a shut-in who'd never even killed a chicken.
[System, pull up current traffic laws. Is there really a provision for executing jaywalkers?]

[Host: Per current municipal traffic code, Article 8 explicitly authorizes law enforcement to stop any red-light violation using martial arts, firearm discharge, or lethal sniper action to neutralize the offender.]
*Nope. Not thinking about this right now. Future me can deal with future problems.*
Jiang Hai saw the light had turned green. He hurried across the street, giving the spreading pool of blood a wide berth, and quickened his pace toward home.
Because the system interface in his mind was flashing a new alert: his first assigned task was about to begin.
